Text:
The UK has already developed a range of sustainable energy initiatives, which will soon be sufficient to provide a stable energy supply—especially when combined with effective, affordable battery technology. This means that our energy will be created here and we will not be dependent on oil and gas supplies from unstable parts of the world.

By contrast, fossil fuels, as well as clearly damaging the environment, support old-fashioned technology that is dirty, dangerous, and has significant impacts on children's health. We see this in the rising rates of asthma and other respiratory diseases.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: Instead of taxing carbon, we should build a full circular waste cycle that rewards responsible choices. Imagine paying for the full recycling cost upfront, then earning back more if you keep products longer or return them for reuse. This encourages durability, reduces packaging waste, and funds local communities. It’s not about punishment, it’s about rewarding good behaviour and making sustainability practical and profitable. Collect at point of sale, use proceeds to invest in disposal. It can be applied across all sectors.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text:
It is now well understood that there is a clear correlation between cutting North Sea production and increasing taxation on producers and the huge increase in energy costs in this country. There are many disbenefits to this approach.
Firstly, energy security. It is insane to pay foreign countries, however benign, for energy that we can produce ourselves. It leaves Britain open to ransom.
Secondly, higher taxation of energy companies will lead them to seek other places to carry out their operations. This knocks on to cutting industrial development in our country. We must change direction!
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text:
The UK has a long history of successfully integrating and supporting people fleeing persecution in other countries.

Many of those people bring skills to the UK from which the UK benefits economically and societally, whether that is Huguenots with weaving and many other crafts in the 17thC, or people from Hong Kong with entrepreneurial and tech skills in the 21stC.

Supporting asylum seekers is morally right—and what goes around comes around—ultimately it is beneficial to everyone to be kind and tolerant.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text: Foreign aid helps both the countries receiving aid and, crucially, those countries giving aid. It is to the benefit of the donee country to lift the country out of a subsistence economy to one where goods can be bought and sold. A market economy also benefits the donor country as providing a market for its goods exported to the donee. As well as economic benefit to both donor and donee, the recipient of aid importantly can improve the lot of citizens so long as the aid given reaches all parts of the population of the donee country. In summary, aid is a win for both the giver and the recipient.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: Oil and gas is not our future, only renewable and nuclear energy can deliver the sustainable future we need. Our energy security is reliant on self-sustainable methods that are less influenced by international markets. Oil and gas only increase the wealth of the wealthy. It's important to understand that UK energy prices are calculated against the highest source, that is presently gas, and those prices drive up our monthly bills and so many other things. To transform our economic troubles, we must invest in renewables and nuclear and allow fossil fuels to leave our lives by the back door.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: We still need to use fossil fuels until we have moved to alternative renewable energy sources. The income from fossil fuels in tax etc. will help fund the renewable sources of energy production. We need to accelerate the process of moving from a fossil fuel economy to one based on renewable energy. The accelerated move to green energy will create jobs and has the potential to make the country a world leader in this sector and could create even more jobs as we could export products and expertise around the world.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: We already import fossil fuels and will continue to do so even if we ban extraction of our own resources. A ban might feel like we will be reducing our CO2 emissions, but that is not the case as our consumption will continue even while gently reducing. So why not have fuel security and a better regulated home industry—often the regulation regime and eco safeguards in some countries is not always reliable. Further, the transportation of overseas extracted fuel itself generates CO2 emissions. And finally, there is the argument of jobs and growth.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: It helps the UK when we increase Foreign Aid spending. Improving the circumstances of all people reduces the risk of war, disease, and global migration. Healthcare reduces risk of global epidemics and improves equality, enabling local economies to become more self-sustaining. Development improves local economy but also reduces pollution, carbon emissions, and climate change. And finally, a large proportion of the spending stays within the UK economy, either in jobs, goods, or research. A cut to foreign aid is not only a cut to the UK economy, it is to increase our global risk.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: Saving the planet for future generations is the most important issue of the day (along with stopping wars). We must stop pollution and the despoiling of our waterways and the damage it causes to the natural world—including wildlife. We have to think about our children and the world we are leaving for them.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: I think that the Government should reduce spending on foreign aid as it promotes dependency rather than encouraging domestic expansion of services and so local production, employment and growth. Further, it is often subject to corruption, theft and misappropriation. Aid should be based on extraordinary need, e.g., famine, natural disaster, war, etc., and improving issues such as education which will lead to more sustained local development of economic growth. Foreign aid also requires a huge bureaucracy to support it with a significant on-cost and is often used for inconsequential projects.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: There is a housing crisis in this country. We cannot accommodate the existing population of the United Kingdom. Of course I understand why perhaps 40 million people from Sudan, which is suffering from a terrible civil war, would like to leave Sudan and come to the UK because English is a second language in Sudan, but we cannot take them all in, not even a fraction of the population. We have taken in millions from the third world into Britain, we cannot continue to do so without destroying community relations. We have seen an increasingly hostile native population towards further immigration.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text:
The UK has made commitments to achieving Net Zero by 2050. We are already in danger of not investing enough in more sustainable solutions to meet this challenge. Money used currently to subsidise fossil fuel extraction should be put to better use investing in more sustainable options with re-training in the fossil fuel sector to transition to the sustainable sector.  
According to the IPCC, our current fossil fuel sector already exceeds the level ensuring that we are able to keep within the 1.5 degrees. With so much housing land at risk of flooding through extreme weather, we cannot justify this.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: I feel the government should reduce spending on foreign aid as the money might not go to the charity or organization to whom it is intended. Without proper research or scrutiny, funds could be used for nefarious actions which were not intended, and may not benefit the communities or organizations intended.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: As a Councillor, I listen to many of my residents expressing the view that even though they have worked really hard all their lives, they are now struggling to make ends meet. They see local services disappearing, school places and doctors' appointments in short supply. They say with the country increasing its debt bill on a daily basis to eye-watering levels, how can we continue to pay other countries monies that we desperately need for UK citizens. We are no longer a rich nation of the past. We face threats from other countries so need to spend more on our defence systems, both military/cyber.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: If we are to tackle concerns about immigration and asylum seekers, we need to encourage greater prosperity and economic security in countries that people are fleeing to come to Britain. People come here because they are desperate and see the possibility of a better life. We cannot lament the level of immigration without helping economic development in less wealthy countries. In addition, we have a duty to help tackle global poverty and suffering. That said, I acknowledge that we do need to focus on what the evidence shows works in the long term, not just doling out money.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text:
As one of the wealthier nations of the world, it is the GB government's moral obligation to send money to help the poorer nations. Even when, as now, we face economic and fiscal problems, we should not turn our backs on those in greater need.
GB spends on defence - killing millions
GB spends on civil servants, government - affecting millions
GB sends arms to help allies - destroying lives

How can we justify reducing humanitarian aid to poorer nations without even thinking to axe aid to save lives and help those in dire need, yet spend freely on destruction?
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: I strongly agree that we should increase spending on Foreign Aid both to support vulnerable people in other countries but also to protect our own interests. As a wealthy nation we have a duty to support people less fortunate than ourselves both financially, medically, and through education and other programmes. As a small island we have limited geographical space to welcome immigrants fleeing war, hunger, and the impact of global warming. Global warming is only going to get worse and this will impact on migration. We need to invest in solutions to reduce the risk, mitigate against climate change.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: We have a responsibility to future generations. We know that temperatures are rising; extreme weather events are causing more damage; and pollution is harming health. Corporations constantly try and avoid their operations; the principle that "the polluter pays" is one we should follow. We owe it to our children.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: Britain really is doing as much as it can, politically, in the current climate. We risk reducing overall support for immigration and asylum—risking drastic measures like leaving the ECHR—if we keep accepting so many, particularly via small boats, which really damages people's sense of fair play. Controlled acceptance of migrants from Westernised countries—Hong Kong, Ukraine—is one thing. Taking a mix of genuine refugees (who are therefore traumatised and likely to be a net drain on resources for a good while) and economic migrants from wildly different cultures is a bridge too far.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text:
As the climate crisis intensifies and global energy markets evolve, it is time to critically reassess the UK’s continued financial commitment to North Sea oil and gas. While historically vital to the nation’s energy security and economy, the sector now represents a diminishing return on investment—both environmentally and economically. Reducing public spending on North Sea fossil fuel extraction is not only a responsible climate action but also a strategic economic pivot toward a sustainable future.
First and foremost, the environmental imperative is clear. The UK has committed to achieving ne
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: The UK suffers financially, with insecurity, and with the human fallout and collapse when foreign institutions fail. A large influx of people seeking a secure life here gives the far right an easy distraction to move debate from policies on services, and on a human level we have an obligation to preserve life abroad after we played such a large role in destabilisation.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: 30% of UK electricity is currently produced from gas and with greater use of electricity (e.g. in EV cars and air sourced central heating) that situation will not change in the immediate future, so we need gas supplies to keep the lights on. It is better for employment to produce our own gas than to import it, so we need to continue gas exploration in the North Sea. The UK contributes a meagre 0.8% to global carbon output; only the big polluters, China, the United States, and India (who together contribute nearly 50% of global CO2 output), can make a real difference.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: We continue to rely on fossil fuels and are likely to do so for some time. Using our own resources we can control the methods of extraction and exploitation of the resources we need, and be more likely to manage or mitigate the ecological impact. We also effectively undercut the ‘less desirable consequences’ that might arise by exporting our needs to the international market. We also secure our own energy—and promote our own economic interests. Finally, we can manage the transition and fund it in a coordinated fashion.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: It's such an enormous topic that it's easy to think that as individuals we can't do anything to reduce emissions. Also, the cost of introducing alternative sources of energy is reported to be very expensive. But even a very small tax on things like plastic packaging in supermarkets could raise funds to help. If it was accompanied by a diktat on big supermarkets putting up the price of food to pass the tax onto the consumer, would you support it? Or better still, reward those supermarkets who transfer to paper packaging by giving them public accolades, would that help?
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: The UK has over years contributed to wars and problems across the world and therefore, we have a responsibility to support those we have helped make homeless or have little access to food. Reducing the world's population by offering contraception is also a really important way to reduce AIDS and unplanned pregnancies where health care is lacking. Using the 0.7% to pay for migrants coming to the UK is counterproductive as it is the UK that has contributed to the issues those migrants have. The UK has values that mean we ought to help those in the developing world.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: If we don't help people in other countries in times of crisis, there is likely to be an increase in instability in the world due to conflicts and the effects of climate change. This will lead to more migration as more parts of the world become difficult or impossible to live in. If there is global instability, then it will affect trade between nations. If we simply put up the drawbridge and isolate ourselves, this is likely to make us poorer as our prosperity depends on trade and partnerships with other countries. It is better for us to help other countries to become better places to live.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: In England the poorest of us are wealthy beyond belief compared to those who have seen their family and friends killed and their homes, livelihood, and communities destroyed. In many cases the reason people want to come here is because they have family and friends here—all too often because the UK 'governed' their country as part of the Empire in the past. The UK has fought its own battles over the past years regarding, for example, homophobia, racism, and religious intolerance—are we not morally and ethically bound to support those who still face such injustice? 1 world, 1 humanity.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text: If people are fleeing wars or persecution, they should seek asylum in the first country they enter, not choose the country that they think will give them the best living, such as the UK. Doing that indicates that the main reason they want to enter the UK is not to seek sanctuary from war or persecution but to gain a better way of life.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text: I believe if consumers could see the extra cost in carbon and money of choosing more polluting options it would enable us all to make better choices for the planet and for our futures. Particularly if concentrated on luxury goods rather than basic food and consumables, it would actually benefit the homegrown UK economy by making those more cost-effective choices. We would also see the real cost of globalization in some of the highly processed items offered to us. As long as those with least to spend were protected, this tax would help counteract greenwashing that makes ethical choices difficult.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: Climate change is real. The earth is warming up due to emissions from human activity. Action to reduce those emissions is essential. Recent hurricanes and tornadoes in the Atlantic and Pacific have wreaked havoc and brought death at unforeseen levels. In Europe, global warming is resulting in much warmer summers, droughts, limited winter frosts, and high winds and rainfall. For our children and grandchildren, we must reduce reliance on fossil fuels. We can start to do this by ceasing oil and gas exploration in the North Sea and developing green energy options. If not, we will destroy our planet.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: A carbon tax will benefit local businesses which feed into the local economy, bringing more jobs and more resilience to people in their communities across the country. Carbon taxes will mean that we will not be dependent on countries such as China, as it will be economically viable to produce goods within our own country. Public transport will be more affordable and energy costs will fall as there will be greater investment in low carbon, renewable energy as well as ways to use less energy such as insulation.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: I think the government should not encourage oil and gas exploration to ensure energy independence because the risks outweigh the benefits. It is far more important to invest in renewables that would reduce our reliance on fossil fuels. It is evident that climate change is real and we are facing ever-increasing extreme weather events. I believe it is incontrovertible that increased greenhouse gas emissions are responsible for this. Additionally, there are risks of pollution from fossil fuel extraction and transport, and it is a dangerous industry for the workers. Invest in safer, cleaner fuels.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text:
Capitalism is the main contributor to global warming. The whole system is flawed and is geared to create profits at the expense of the planet’s resources. We have to rapidly move away from reliance on fossil fuels. We have to go back to local, seasonal, and organic wherever possible to take profit out of our lives. I fear for my grandchildren’s future because the elite have been making profit from the masses for too long. History will damn this generation because we know it’s not working and our societal breakdown will be on our heads. Inaction is not available now.  
It begins with honesty.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: Encouraging responsible oil and gas exploration in the North Sea strengthens our energy independence during a period of global instability. While we expand renewables, we still rely on steady, affordable supplies to keep homes warm and businesses running. Using our own regulated resources reduces reliance on foreign states with weaker environmental standards, protecting both our security and our transition to cleaner energy.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: As a town we have long been a place of sanctuary, we take pride in our multicultural community. When we come together and really talk, we find that the majority of us have a wider global history than our skin tone might suggest. We forget that those who had the courage to seek asylum are those who had the courage, skills, the wherewithal to plan and carry out a hazardous journey. We need a system that welcomes and enables people via safe routes, so that they arrive ready to contribute, grow and build their family lives. We are all part of the global family.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text: We need to build a safer world where everyone is born with a chance to have a fulfilling life. Some of us were lucky to be born in a relatively affluent part of the world. We should help those who are not. If you don't agree with this, think of it as an insurance policy. Help those in need and they will be more likely to help us when we are in need.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text:
We need warm, healthy homes and low energy bills.  
We need to produce our own clean energy, and be independent of dictators and oil barons who can charge what they like for our energy supplies, or worse still, cut them off.  
We need clean air, clean rivers and a stable climate.  
Fossil fuels have been subsidised with your money, to make profits for billionaires and foreign despots. By taxing things for the carbon they produce, we can shift the market to provide the clean energy systems we need, stop polluting our air and rivers, and ultimately provide free fuel for all from renewables.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: What is the nation's favourite drink? It may seem a trivial question when we consider the important issue of the foreign aid budget, but consider it for a moment. Tea. A plant we cannot effectively cultivate in the UK. A plant we came to love due to our nation's colonial history. We once controlled 1/4 of the planet. And we are not a nation that would choose to ignore nations we once called ours. The shift from colonial power to commonwealth ally is the shift that saw Britain use its power and resources to help nations that we once led, to nations that became our allies in the face of global
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: Foreign aid is our opportunity to bring other places in the world up to our standard. Decreasing foreign aid increases economic migration. This means more people looking for small boats to cross the Channel, an overloaded asylum system where those fleeing war cannot get their applications processed, and criminals getting rich from exploitation. If we truly want to live in a world where people are happy to stay in their own country, we should be increasing foreign aid to fund long-term projects enabling genuine development in the world's poorest countries.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: Reducing aid any further from 0.3% would undermine the UK's long-term strategic interests. Foreign aid creates stability in fragile nations, whether through food assistance or conflict prevention programs, which reduces the need for far more expensive responses later. Foreign aid is not charity; it is strategic, stabilizing, and profoundly effective soft diplomacy. Reducing aid would weaken the UK precisely at the moment when strong international relationships matter most. When countries like the UK become secure and prosperous, we benefit from reduced migration pressures and fewer security threats.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: Britain has a long and proud history of offering sanctuary during periods of upheaval and persecution around the world. Where genuine asylum seekers need our help, we have a duty to protect them. The world is increasingly volatile, through war and climate change, and where groups are being unfairly targeted, Britain should stand ready to help. We stepped up during the Holocaust, the genocide in Kosovo, and most recently in supporting the evacuation of Afghanistan. Families are still together and are now thriving because of Britain’s interventions. We should be proud of our record.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text: This country has a long tradition of sheltering those who are being persecuted at home, or who find themselves in war zones, and welcoming such strangers is a part of our core British values. British society has been enriched by immigration, not diminished by it. It should be possible for applications to be made from overseas, as it was in the past. Asylum seekers should be allowed to work, and pay taxes, so as to make a solid contribution to our country. Applications for asylum should be processed thoroughly and quickly, and those whose applications are declined should be removed promptly.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text:
We look after our own people first, we can't afford to give money away at this time, when our infrastructure like roads are so bad. We are short of ambulances, need more local hospitals to take the pressure off the main hospital.  
All old people homes should be run by the state and not private, bring back sports at schools, to stop the children getting overweight.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: Due to international and external influences, UK Ltd should become as self-sufficient as possible in the short term but also legislate on time limits for any investment. The resources should belong to the UK and private industry, and the UK government should set the barrel price and not rely on the international market to set prices.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text:
Immigration is driven by forces that prevail in the country of origin: conflict, oppression, climate issues, or economic challenges, among others.  
To address domestic issues like asylum seekers or refugees, then improving the living conditions in areas of the world that face these challenges would go a long way to achieving those aims.  
Poverty, lack of clean water, and government oppression for characteristics that would be protected in the UK are things that can be helped by targeted international aid as well as better health care and education. This would benefit the UK and generate goodwill.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text:
Not all who want to make their economic lives better should be allowed to come to the UK. Those who have been punished by their government for standing up for others, or tortured because they hold religious views different from those in the country they are fleeing, should have a safe route to apply for asylum—they don't—so have to try to come on a small rubber boat.  
The UK gave 750,000 people per year legal permits to come here and work. 40,000, or 1/20th that number, came on small boats, and half of those have been sent home.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text: The UK produces 1% of the pollutants affecting climate change. To develop new taxes will alienate rather than encourage polluters. Taxing does not solve problems; you have to persuade people of the reasons for a course of action. Donald Trump alone has increased global warming with all the bombs he has dropped and given others a reason to drop. We are told that we should eat vegetarian to reduce emissions, but nobody tells us how many emissions are created by food processing to make vegetables taste like the meat dishes they replace.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: I will suggest there is a marked difference between those whom the UK chooses to accept for humanitarian purposes and those who choose to enter the UK illegally, potentially as economic migrants. The UK, as one of the wealthiest economies in the world, is a tranquil corner of our increasingly turbulent planet and to provide safety and sanctuary for those fleeing war or persecution should be admitted. Most people fleeing persecution want to remain in their own countries and we should make every effort to accommodate them until such time as it is deemed safe for them to return.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text: I think the government should reduce spending on foreign aid because many of the countries are already receiving aid from other countries on a major scale. Several of these countries are self-sufficient and the thought that we are supporting them I do not think the electorate would support. A lot of the aid in the past has gone to nonproductive situations including nuclear options. It was provided in the first place for humanitarian reasons not for war. In addition, we have too many problems of our own to be spending money that we can ill afford to do. How much is HS2 finally going to cost?
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: As society comes under more pressure from a changing world and climate, we look to our own... charity starts at home. As a local councillor, I have to represent my area, which is under threat from the changing weather. The majority of my residents are conservative with a small 'c', with a higher than usual age profile. My people are getting more concerned about what is to come. We can all feel for and have sympathy for migrants, but if the numbers are too large, the change too rapid, people will feel threatened.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text: A substantial amount of my Ph.D. research was on the Holocaust. I interviewed heroic women who smuggled Jews out of Germany and France, only to have problems saving them in Switzerland. It is important to understand the human cost both for society and for individual families. My mother-in-law was blessed with blonde hair and blue eyes or she would have perished with other Jewish children. Statistics are hard to digest, but individuals speak. You yourself might face persecution one day. We need a strong framework of international law, humanitarian aid, and British ethics. Refugees contribute much.
Issue: accept more asylum seekers who are fleeing war or persecution.

Text:
Introducing a carbon tax will not be a fair and equitable way of trying to reduce the amount we pollute.

The biggest problem is the producers of these products; they are the ones who should pay. Taxing them directly and subsidising greener options would be far better.

With the cost of living already putting pressures on households, I don’t think adding another tax will solve the problem and will create more hardship for families. It will likely be a regressive form of tax too.

If something is bad for the environment, just ban it. Starting with plastic.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text:
Everyday goods are used by "everyday" people: put simply, those without large financial or time resources and often without the option of making different choices. A tax of this kind will push up prices for everyone, but impact the poorest in society the most.
Rather, we should be looking to incentivise green behaviour by providing benefits to those making carbon reductions in their day-to-day lives.
The only exception here would be for goods over a high threshold cost—i.e. where we can be confident that increased prices due to higher taxation would hit the most well off.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: North Sea oil does not affect either the security or the price of oil in the UK, because it's drilled by private companies and is sold entirely on world markets. Most of it leaves the country immediately. The amount is too small to have any impact on prices. New drilling would take decades to do, would cost more than previous (easily accessible) fields and would probably be irrelevant by the time it came ashore because of renewables. Gas production is better (about 60% consumed locally) so is a stronger case. But there aren't many gas fields left to develop so it is not significant long term.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: People flee from famine and war. In many cases they try to come to the UK, putting pressure on our public services. If we helped prevent famine and conflict through our aid work, it would ease the pressure on our borders and our public services. It also gives us the authority to influence other international donors to act in what is ultimately the UK national interest.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: It is imperative for the UK and the world that we reduce carbon emissions to prevent a climate disaster. This significant contribution to doing so will not only help do so, but also ensure that investment goes into creating the jobs and industries of the future, providing a major economic benefit to the UK.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: Government should tax bad things and use the money to invest in good things, it's that simple! We know that carbon emissions are causing the climate crisis, and we see the terrible effects already: devastation due to flooding, wildfires, etc. So we need to incentivize business to produce goods in a more climate-friendly way. A carbon tax will do this. And the government can then use the revenue to invest in all the good stuff it should be doing: cutting child poverty, building the infrastructure we need, building the fairer and greener country our children deserve.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: In order to achieve net zero and protect standards of living, we need a stable energy supply through the transition. Without it, we will have to buy in supplies from the Middle East; the carbon footprint of bringing it here will be huge. It will damage the planet more and make us vulnerable both to energy price hikes and foreign interference.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: I would first want to understand the person's reasons for disagreeing and ensure I listened to it. We can both agree that we want to reduce the number of small boats and people coming to the UK by irregular means. Often people are coming because of issues in their own country and they see better prospects in the UK. Without helping their home countries, we will never change that perception and people will continue to come. In addition, this makes our country less safe, because often, when the UK withdraws, other countries like China and Russia step in or extremists like the Taliban.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: This would benefit our country by reducing immigration, would increase our standing in the world, and provide even more power to our diplomatic abilities. By increasing spending on foreign aid, we would be able to tackle the growing threat of China and Russia in continents such as Africa, the Middle East, and Eastern Europe, which would make our country much safer.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: The UK has a proud history of supporting some of the poorest people in the world, including educating girls, tackling climate change and building schools. As the fifth largest economy on the globe, we have a responsibility towards the poorest in the world. The broadest shoulders should support the weaker. As a founding member of the United Nations, we must help other nations to support people with medication, education and climate change.
Issue: reduce spending on foreign aid.

Text: The proposed carbon tax income will be used to directly support communities that are suffering with poor levels of air quality, access to public transport, and flooding. The carbon tax will not be a carbon credit scheme as we do not wish for the capital markets to make money on the back of our communities.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text:
Why not use our reserves to cut prices and reduce our reliance on other states? I'm afraid it's because the detail shows it does neither.

Very little of the type of oil and gas in the North Sea can be used in cars or power stations, none from new licenses will be extracted before we need to be CO2 neutral, and gas is already the most expensive form of electricity generation.

The UK has the potential to be a clean energy superpower, driving down bills and exporting overseas, while avoiding the increasing heat which will drive up migration and increase the risk of conflict. So, let's do that.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: We have the chance to create a better future for ourselves and our children. That future will have less air pollution, won't be affected by climate breakdown, and there will be energy security for all, with jobs in good, clean industries for us all. But we can only create that future if we invest in it—by putting money into renewables and alternatives to oil and gas. Fossil fuels are decreasing resources—they become more expensive to continue using, meaning our energy bills won't come down. We must choose carefully how we use our resources now so the future can benefit.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: The UK benefits from a bounty in the North Sea. We already have the infrastructure, companies, regulatory regime, safety regime, and highly skilled workforce to access the remaining bounty. Extracting the oil and gas that remains in the North Sea is kinder to the planet than having to bring it in from other countries: countries that are often located thousands of miles away and countries that do not have the health and safety or human rights record that the UK has. The dangers in the world are increasing with conflict—why would the UK put itself at risk of diminished energy security?
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text:
It is vital to retain jobs in the North Sea in order to keep the skills for a just transition to net zero and to ensure our energy independence during a time of global instability.

If we shut down North Sea production too soon, we will have to import oil and gas from elsewhere, which would add to our carbon footprint, may be fraught to secure supply, and will guarantee jobs overseas and not here.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text: The costs of not exploiting oil and gas reserves are massive in terms of jobs and money. Latest estimates show that, by not pursuing this policy, thousands of jobs will be lost and £5 billion will be lost across Scotland. That means we have far less to invest in our NHS, education and social services. That means communities will be left behind with no just transition. This policy is entirely compatible with our Net Zero targets and our renewables expansion—particularly offshore wind.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.

Text:
Our current carbon emissions trajectory means that global warming will lead to environmental disaster, with flooding and irretrievable loss of life and species. We need to start making some practical changes to the way we lead our lives, to cause less environmental harm.
Currently, if our actions harm the environment, then the poorest nations of the world will suffer more of the impacts (because of their geography).
A more fair distribution of cause and effect would be to ask the polluter to pay.
Polluter pays means that as a society we should front-load the cost of pollution to those causing harm.
Issue: introduce a carbon tax on everyday goods and energy that pollute more to reduce emissions.

Text: We depend on fossil fuels. If we fail to drill in the North Sea and open up new gas & oil fields, we are at the mercy of foreign suppliers and the world market. We can issue new licences to allow further exploration with zero risk to the UK. Our friends in Norway are already doing this and discovering untapped sources. The current war in the Middle East has exacerbated the situation and demonstrates the short-sighted approach that we are taking presently to open up new sources.
Issue: encourage oil and gas exploration in the North Sea to ensure energy independence.
